skill.name: pdb-database
skill.description: Access RCSB PDB structure data for proteins and nucleic acids. Search entries, retrieve metadata, and download PDB or mmCIF coordinate files.
skill.summary: Find, retrieve, and download RCSB PDB structure data for structural biology workflows.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, sensitive-data, weak-crypto, and heuristic signals. Review found the command alerts are mostly Markdown code-fence or inline-code false positives, while the real behaviors are public RCSB API calls, coordinate-file downloads, local writes, and one hardcoded curl subprocess example. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Legitimate Public RCSB Network Access
low_findings.description: The skill documents HTTP requests to official RCSB PDB endpoints for search, metadata retrieval, and coordinate downloads. This is expected for the skill purpose, and no credential transmission or unrelated external destination was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network calls are clearly tied to official RCSB PDB URLs and documented API workflows. I found no evidence of secrets, tokens, or private data being sent to external endpoints.

low_findings.title: Local File Writes for Structure Downloads
low_findings.description: Examples write downloaded PDB and mmCIF files to local paths. This is a normal workflow for structure analysis, but users should choose safe output directories and avoid overwriting important files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are direct and visible in download examples. The context is benign, but the helper accepts an output directory, so normal filesystem caution still applies.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Curl Subprocess Debug Example
low_findings.description: The reference includes a Python subprocess.run call that invokes curl against a fixed RCSB URL. The command has hardcoded arguments and no user-controlled shell string, so command injection risk is low.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is explicit and uses an argument list with a fixed URL. There is no shell=True use or concatenated user input in the shown example.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives from Markdown and Scientific Terms
low_findings.description: Most Ruby backtick execution alerts refer to Markdown code fences or inline code. The Windows SAM and weak-cryptography alerts are triggered by biological sequence text, PDB terminology, and API documentation, not credential stores or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are documentation, code blocks, URLs, or protein sequence strings. I found no Windows SAM database access, weak crypto implementation, or Ruby shell execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Requests to Public RCSB APIs
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples use requests and documented URLs to retrieve public structure data from RCSB PDB services.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present, but all visible destinations are official RCSB services. No unrelated collection endpoint or credential payload was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Downloaded Data Written to Disk
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples write PDB and mmCIF response bodies to local files for downstream structural analysis.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write operations are explicit and limited to downloaded structure files in the examples. There is no evidence of reading or modifying sensitive local files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess Invocation for Curl Debugging
dangerous_patterns.description: The API reference demonstrates subprocess.run with curl for a fixed RCSB REST endpoint.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess invocation is present, but the command is fixed and does not use shell interpolation. It remains a pattern users should review before execution.

content.value_statement: Finding reliable macromolecular structure data can require several RCSB tools and file formats. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through PDB searches, metadata retrieval, and coordinate downloads.
actual_capabilities: Search RCSB PDB entries by text, attributes, sequence similarity, and structure similarity.
Retrieve entry, polymer entity, assembly, ligand, and experimental metadata from RCSB APIs.
Download coordinate files in PDB, mmCIF, BinaryCIF, and biological assembly formats.
Build batch workflows for multiple PDB IDs with rate-limit guidance.
Support structural biology, drug discovery, protein engineering, education, and visualization tasks.
limitations: Requires network access to RCSB PDB services for live searches and downloads.
Does not validate scientific conclusions or replace expert structural biology review.
Downloaded files can be large and must be stored in user-approved directories.
API availability, rate limits, and schema fields can change outside the skill.

use_cases.title: Find Drug Target Structures
use_cases.description: Search for protein-ligand complexes, filter by quality metrics, and download coordinates for modeling.

use_cases.title: Compare Homologous Proteins
use_cases.description: Run sequence or structure similarity searches, collect matching PDB IDs, and summarize metadata.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Examples
use_cases.description: Retrieve well-known structures, explain file formats, and gather metadata for classroom visualization.

prompt_templates.title: Search for a Protein
prompt_templates.prompt: Search RCSB PDB for hemoglobin structures. Return representative PDB IDs, experimental methods, and resolutions.

prompt_templates.title: Filter High Quality Entries
prompt_templates.prompt: Find human kinase structures solved by X-ray diffraction with resolution better than 2.0 angstroms. Summarize the best candidates.

prompt_templates.title: Download Coordinates Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Download the mmCIF file for PDB ID 4HHB into a project data directory. Explain the file contents after download.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Batch Analysis Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a rate-limited workflow to search ATP-bound structures, fetch metadata, rank by resolution, and prepare coordinate downloads.

output_examples.input: Search for hemoglobin structures with high resolution.
output_examples.output: A ranked shortlist of hemoglobin PDB entries with titles, methods, resolution values, organisms, and recommended next steps.
A concise structure summary covering title, experimental method, resolution, deposition date, polymer entities, and useful download formats.

output_examples.input: Plan a batch download for selected kinase structures.
output_examples.output: A staged workflow with input PDB IDs, preferred file format, rate-limit handling, output directory guidance, and validation checks.

best_practices: Confirm PDB IDs and file formats before downloading coordinate files.
Use rate limits, caching, and small batches for repeated RCSB API requests.
Store downloaded structures in a dedicated project directory with clear filenames.
anti_patterns: Do not run broad batch downloads without rate limiting or a clear research need.
Do not treat database metadata as proof of biological interpretation without review.
Do not write downloaded files into system directories or untrusted paths.

faq.question: What database does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses RCSB Protein Data Bank resources for public macromolecular structure data and metadata.

faq.question: Can it download coordinate files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide downloads for PDB, mmCIF, BinaryCIF, and biological assembly files.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Yes. Live searches, metadata retrieval, and coordinate downloads require access to RCSB services.

faq.question: Can it analyze downloaded structures?
faq.answer: It can guide parsing and basic analysis with structural biology libraries, but expert review is still needed.

faq.question: Is it suitable for drug discovery workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports target lookup, ligand complex searches, quality filtering, and coordinate preparation.

faq.question: What should users watch for?
faq.answer: Users should manage rate limits, confirm file destinations, and validate scientific conclusions independently.
